query: definition of lucid interval in head injury

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Trauma (Archived) -- Evaluation -- Clinical Decision Rules
retrieved_docs.content: Perhaps the most widely known and best-studied rule is the Canadian head CT rule. The Canadian head CT rule has inclusion and exclusion criteria. Patients to whom this rule applies are those with GCS 13 to 15 with loss of consciousness, amnesia to the head injury event, or witnessed disorientation. Patients who are less than 16 years old, are on blood-thinning medications or have a seizure after the injury are excluded from this decision rule. The Canadian head CT rule puts forth the following high-risk criteria: GCS less than 15 at 2 hours post-injury, suspected open or depressed skull fracture, any signs of basilar skull fracture (hemotympanum, raccoon eyes, Battle sign, CSF otorrhea or rhinorrhea, two or more episodes of emesis, and age 65 years or older. Medium risk criteria include retrograde amnesia to the event of 30 minutes or greater, or a dangerous mechanism (pedestrian struck by motor vehicle, occupant ejected from a motor vehicle, or fall from over 3 feet or more than five stairs). If the patient is a candidate for the application of the rule and has no high or medium risk criteria, CT is not a recommendation. The sensitivity of this rule is 83 to 100% for all traumatic intracranial findings and 100% for findings requiring neurosurgical intervention. If either of the medium-risk criteria is positive, CT merits consideration, and if any of the high-risk criteria are positive, the decision rule cannot rule out the need for imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Abusive Head Trauma -- Introduction -- Abusive Head Trauma
retrieved_docs.content: The American Academy of Pediatrics and the CDC define AHT as intentional injury to the intracranial contents or skull of a pediatric patient (0–5 years of age) resulting from blunt force, forceful shaking involving acceleration, deceleration, and rotational movements, or a combination of these forces. This definition emphasizes the nature of the injury rather than the specific causative mechanism. Additional terminology includes the following: SBS Battered child [10] Whiplash shaken infant syndrome [11] Nonaccidental trauma Intentional head trauma Inflicted head trauma Brain injuries are the most common cause of traumatic death in children younger than 2 years and remain the leading cause of injury during infancy. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Axonal Injury -- History and Physical -- Clinical Presentation
retrieved_docs.content: DAI is fundamentally a clinical diagnosis that is typically suspected whenever a patient sustains a high-energy, rotational-acceleration head trauma and remains comatose (GCS ≤8) for longer than 6 hours without a lucid interval, and without a mass lesion or midline shift on imaging. DAI is very unlikely to happen in falls from the patient's height. The deceleration in this case is not sufficient to cause DAI, but might lead to other types of TBI, eg, subdural hematomas and skull fractures. A lucid interval can occur in DAI, but its likelihood diminishes as histopathologic grade increases. In fact, Adams and colleagues reported that none of the patients with severe, macroscopically evident DAI exhibited a lucid interval. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Traumatic Brain Injury -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: TBI severity is classified into three categories: mild, moderate, and severe. Mild TBI or concussion is clinically defined as a loss of consciousness lasting <30 minutes, post-traumatic amnesia lasting <24 hrs, or any alteration of consciousness. Moderate or severe TBI is clinically defined as a loss of consciousness lasting ≥ 30 minutes to prolonged coma, post-traumatic amnesia lasting ≥24 hours up to permanently, or a Glasgow Coma score as low as 3.

retrieved_docs.title: Traumatic Brain Injury -- Evaluation -- New Orleans Criteria
retrieved_docs.content: Headache Vomiting (any) Age > 60 years Drug or alcohol intoxication Seizure Trauma visible above clavicles Short-term memory deficits

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Axonal Injury -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A definitive diagnosis of DAI can be made through postmortem pathological examination of brain tissue. However, in clinical practice, a diagnosis of DAI is made by implementing clinical information and radiographic findings. Understanding the mechanism of head injury facilitates a differential diagnosis of DAI. Patients who experience rotational or acceleration-deceleration closed head injury should be suspected of having DAI. Generally, DAI is diagnosed after a TBI with a GCS <8 for more than 6 consecutive hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Vascular Extremity Trauma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: As with all trauma patients, advanced trauma life support (ATLS) protocol should be followed as with any patient that presents to the trauma bay.  After completion of airway and breathing evaluation, assessment of circulation is next.  The management of vascular injuries has its basis the concept of hard or soft signs.  The WTA and EAST each have different definitions of hard signs and soft signs.  The WTA defines hard signs as expanding hematoma, bruit, thrill, external bleeding, pulselessness, pallor, paresthesia, paralysis or pain. The WTA recommends using the hard signs for major named arteries, which includes anything proximal to the anterior tibial artery or tibioperoneal bifurcation but it excludes the profunda femoris artery.  The EAST defines hard signs as pulsatile bleeding, thrill, pulse deficit, bruit or expanding hematoma.  The definition of soft signs, per the WTA, is a history of arterial bleeding at the scene or in transit, neurological deficit occurring in a nerve adjacent to a name artery, proximity of the injury to name artery or a small nonpulsatile hematoma present over an artery.  The EAST definition of soft signs is the same as the WTA. [1] [10] The finding of a single soft sign has approximately a 10% rate of vascular injury.  In comparison, two or more soft signs can have a vascular injury rate of 25%. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Revised Trauma Scale -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous studies demonstrated the benefits of utilizing the RTS. Researchers found that after implementation of the RTS, there was no difference in the error rate for diagnosis and investigation compared to not utilizing the RTS; however, there was a statistically significant improvement concerned with the appropriateness of the formulated treatments. [7] [8] Using the RTS as a triage tool reduced the treatment errors from 21 out of 36 patients (58%) to 14 out of 54 patients (26%). [8] Additionally, researchers found that the RTS effectively predicted mortality for patients with an RTS greater than 7. [9] Furthermore, the RTS was most useful in predicting the survival rates among patients with head injuries compared to patients with penetrating or blunt traumas. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Axonal Injury -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Concussion/postconcussive syndrome Distinguishing DAI from concussion remains challenging, in part because the traditional 6-hour loss-of-consciousness (LOC) threshold lacks strong empirical support, and axonal lesions have been documented in patients with shorter LOC intervals. Clinically, concussion is defined by transient symptoms (eg, headache, confusion, brief LOC of <6 hours) without radiographic abnormalities, whereas DAI is presumed when LOC exceeds 6 hours and is supported by white-matter lesions on imaging. However, conventional MRI detects fewer than half of DAI lesions, many of which are microscopic or nonhemorrhagic and therefore occult on routine scans. DTI) and tractography significantly improve sensitivity by revealing microstructural disruptions in the corpus callosum, brainstem, and significant fiber tracts, but these findings may also occur in so-called “mild” TBI injuries. Neither LOC duration nor standard MRI alone reliably separates concussion from DAI; integration of prolonged neurologic dysfunction, advanced diffusion imaging, and careful exclusion of confounders (sedation effects, prior injuries) is essential for accurate diagnosis. [22] However, clinicians should be aware that, for clinical purposes, the 2 conditions can overlap, and attempting to distinguish them does not currently have clinical significance.

retrieved_docs.title: Geriatric Head Injury -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Patients older than 60 years old are a growing demographic in the developed world and are at higher risk for traumatic injury.  The definition of "elderly" or "geriatric" is a controversial topic.  Many articles base the definition of "geriatric" at age 65, but for the most part, this does not have a basis in evidence-based medicine.  Age 65 is commonly used as a default; however, evidence supports that a patient's preexisting conditions and comorbidities may be a better predictor of long term complications secondary to trauma. [3] These comorbidities in older patients make them more susceptible to falls, such as in unilateral weakness secondary to stroke.  Also, elderly patients commonly take more medications, which can precipitate falls, cause confusion, or worsen bleeding.  For example, aspirin or even more potent forms of anticoagulation, such as coumadin, can drastically worsen traumatic intracranial bleeds.  These chronic conditions compounded by the effects of polypharmacy cause elderly patients to have less capacity to compensate for traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Trauma (Archived) (Nursing) -- Evaluation -- Clinical Decision Rules
retrieved_docs.content: Perhaps the most widely known and best-studied rule is the Canadian head CT rule. The Canadian head CT rule has inclusion and exclusion criteria. Patients to whom this rule applies are those with GCS 13-15 with loss of consciousness, amnesia to the head injury event, or witnessed disorientation. Patients who are less than 16 years old, are on blood-thinning medications or have a seizure after the injury get excluded from this decision rule. The Canadian head CT rule puts forth the following high-risk criteria: GCS under 15 at 2 hours post-injury, suspected open or depressed skull fracture, any signs of basilar skull fracture (hemotympanum, raccoon eyes, Battle sign, CSF otorrhea or rhinorrhea, 2 or more episodes of emesis, and age 65 years or older. Medium risk criteria include retrograde amnesia to the event of 30 minutes or greater, or a dangerous mechanism (pedestrian struck by motor vehicle, occupant ejected from a motor vehicle, or fall from more than 3 feet or over five stairs). If the patient is a candidate for the application of the rule and has no high or medium risk criteria, CT is not recommended. The sensitivity of this rule is 83 to 100% for all traumatic intracranial findings and 100% for findings requiring neurosurgical intervention. If either of the medium-risk criteria is positive, CT should be considered, and if any of the high-risk criteria are positive, the decision rule cannot rule out the need for imaging.

retrieved_docs.title: Revised Trauma Scale -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple studies found few disadvantages associated with including capillary refill and respiratory expansion within the scoring system. [7] First responders shared challenges observing the capillary refill and respiratory expansion during dark settings or at night. Another group coordinated through the American College of Surgeons Committee on Trauma found that the TS may underestimate head injury severity among such cohorts. These factors led to the formulation of the Revised Trauma Scale (RTS), which removed the capillary refill and respiratory expansion. Additionally, 2 versions of the RTS were created; one was developed specifically for triage (T-RTS), and the other was developed for outcome evaluations and controlling for injury severity (RTS). [7] When providers utilize the RTS for triage, they use raw values, and when using the assessment tool for outcome evaluations, the coded values are weighted and summed. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Concussion -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A concussion is a “traumatically induced transient disturbance of brain function.” [1] Concussions are a subset of the neurologic injuries known as traumatic brain injuries. Traumatic brain injuries have varying severity, ranging from mild, transient symptoms to extended periods of altered consciousness. Given the usually self-limited nature of symptoms associated with a concussion, the term mild traumatic brain injury (mTBI) is often used interchangeably to refer to a concussion. However, concussions are technically a subset of mTBIs. The prognosis is usually good, and most patients experience complete resolution of symptoms. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified site injury is a broad injury category that encompasses more specific conditions such as intracranial injury, indicating that injuries without a clearly defined location may include trauma to the brain. This category is primarily derived from the concept of multiple injuries (إصابات متعددة), reflecting that when several injuries occur together or the exact site is not specified, they are often grouped under a general “unspecified site” label. Recognizing that unspecified site injury can represent both intracranial trauma and a collection of multiple injuries helps clinicians adopt a comprehensive assessment approach, ensuring that potential brain involvement is not overlooked and that the overall injury burden is appropriately addressed in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the knowledge network unifies head and neck trauma, intracranial and skull‑base injuries, all skeletal fractures, their associated tissue damage, poisoning mechanisms, external causes (MVT, aircraft crash), procedural treatments, and the spectrum of late‑effect sequelae under the central concepts of fracture and late effects of unspecified injury, providing a comprehensive framework for injury classification, management, and long‑term patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1715
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral injury due to birth trauma is recognized as a specific manifestation of birth trauma, indicating that this type of brain injury occurs as a result of traumatic events during delivery. In medical terminology, the concept of birth trauma corresponds to the Arabic phrase “نزيف مخي وليدي,” linking the English description of traumatic birth‑related injuries to its equivalent expression in Arabic. This relationship underscores that cerebral injury caused by birth trauma is a subset of the broader category of birth trauma and that the term is directly mapped to its Arabic counterpart for cross‑linguistic clinical reference.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2283
retrieved_docs.content: Antemortem injury is classified under the category of Unspecified site injury, indicating that the injury occurred before death but its precise anatomical location is not defined. Unspecified site injury serves as a broader classification that includes more specific injury types such as intracranial injury, reflecting its role as an overarching term for injuries without a clearly identified site. The concept of Unspecified site injury is primarily derived from the Arabic phrase “إصابات متعددة,” which translates to “multiple injuries,” emphasizing that it encompasses a range of injury presentations that are not limited to a single location. This hierarchical relationship helps clinicians and researchers organize injury data, linking general, location‑unspecified injuries to both specific intracranial injuries and to the broader context of antemortem trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 529
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning / injury is positioned as a subcategory of intracranial injury (إصابة داخل الجمجمة), indicating that certain poisoning or injury events are regarded as forms of head trauma. Within the intracranial injury hierarchy, specific conditions are identified as direct subtypes: subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness, fracture of anterior fossa, fracture of middle fossa, and concussion (الارتجاج المخي). The subarachnoid hemorrhage condition is both classified as an intracranial injury and associated with internal injury (إصابة داخلية), reflecting its link to deeper bodily damage. Concussion (الارتجاج المخي) is also related to sports injury (إصابة رياضية), highlighting its frequent occurrence in athletic contexts. In addition, Poisoning / injury includes superficial injury as a child concept, and superficial injury further encompasses corneal abrasion (خدش القرنية) as a specific manifestation. This integrated framework demonstrates how various traumatic and toxic events are organized from broad injury categories down to precise clinical entities, supporting comprehensive assessment and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 994
retrieved_docs.content: Intracranial injury (إصابة داخل الجمجمة) is the Arabic term that classifies the English concept of intracranial injury and serves as a broad category for several specific head trauma entities. Within this category, traumatic brain edema (Traumatisch hersenoedeem) is identified as a child of intracranial injury and simultaneously as a subtype of brain edema (أوديما دماغية). Fracture of the anterior fossa and fracture of the middle fossa are each described as “isa” (i.e., specific instances) of intracranial injury, indicating that injuries to these cranial compartments are considered forms of intracranial trauma. Additionally, the complex lesion “Closed fracture of vault of skull with cerebral laceration AND/OR contusion” is linked to both the general Arabic term for skull fracture (كسر في الجمجمة) and to intracranial injury, reflecting that this condition involves a skull fracture that penetrates the vault and produces direct cerebral damage. Together, these relationships illustrate how various traumatic brain conditions—edema, fissure-specific fractures, and combined skull‑vault fractures with brain laceration or contusion—are organized under the overarching concept of intracranial injury, highlighting their interconnections for clinical classification and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2662
retrieved_docs.content: The term hlava (head) is linked to إصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury), indicating that injuries involving the head fall within the category of intracranial trauma. Both Fracture of anterior fossa and Fracture of middle fossa are identified as specific types of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, illustrating that fractures of the anterior and middle cranial fossae represent distinct forms of intracranial injury. This relationship underscores that damage to the skull base, such as these fossa fractures, is encompassed by the broader concept of intracranial injury affecting the head.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2104
retrieved_docs.content: The head (hlava) is directly related to intracranial injury (إصابة داخل الجمجمة). Both Fracture of anterior fossa and Fracture of middle fossa are classified as types of intracranial injury (إصابة داخل الجمجمة). Additionally, closed head injury (إصابة الرأس المغلقة) is also categorized under intracranial injury (إصابة داخل الجمجمة). These connections illustrate that various specific head trauma conditions, including anterior and middle fossa fractures and closed head injuries, are encompassed within the broader concept of intracranial injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 205
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning / injury is the overarching category that includes both intracranial injuries (إصابة داخل الجمجمة) and fractures (كسر). Within intracranial injuries, “Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness” is classified as an internal injury (إصابة داخلية) and as a closed head injury (إِصابة الرأس المغلقة). Specific fracture entities such as “Fracture of anterior fossa,” “Fracture of middle fossa,” and “Fracture in the cranial vault” (كسر في قبو القحف) are mapped to the Arabic term “كسر في قبو القحف” and linked to the general concept of fracture (كسر). The Dutch entry “Fractuur van schedel of aangezichtsbeenderen, deel niet gespecificeerd” is a parent of كسر في قبو القحف, with its finding site identified as facial bones (obličejové kosti). retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury), indicating that this form of hemorrhagic brain trauma often co‑occurs with internal injuries and should be evaluated together in clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: Antemortem injury is classified under the broader “Unspecified site injury” (Arabic إصابات متعددة), which is a child of the general “injury” (إصابة) concept and is linked to intracranial injury (إصابة داخل الجمجمة). Intracranial injury is treated as a specific type of wound (جرح) and includes entities such as “Fracture of anterior fossa” and “Fracture of middle fossa,” both identified as “إصابة داخل الجمجمة” and as subtypes of the general fracture (كسر) and skull fracture (كسر في الجمجمة). The entry “Closed fracture of vault of skull with cerebral laceration AND/OR contusion” is a child of both “كسر في الجمجمة” and “intracranial injury,” reinforcing the connection between skull fractures and intracranial damage. The classification system also contains the contextual category “Circumstances Related to Injury or Death,” whose children include assault (الاعتداء) and self‑harm (تشويه الذات); both are linked to the wound (جرح) concept, indicating that injuries arising from these

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 796
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of vault of skull with cerebral laceration AND/OR contusion is recognized as a specific form of كسر في الجمجمة (skull fracture) and simultaneously falls under the broader category of intracranial injury. This dual classification underscores that a closed fracture of the skull vault accompanied by brain laceration or contusion involves both disruption of the cranial bone and direct injury to the brain tissue, guiding clinicians to address skeletal and neural components together in diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 799
retrieved_docs.content: Antemortem injury is conceptually linked to an Unspecified site injury, indicating that injuries occurring before death can be categorized when the exact anatomical location is not defined. This Unspecified site injury is considered a broader form of intracranial injury and is primarily derived from the Arabic term “إصابات متعددة” (multiple injuries), reflecting that it often represents a collection of diverse traumatic findings. Antemortem injury also encompasses the category Circumstances Related to Injury or Death, which further subdivides into specific contexts such as “الاعتداء” (assault) and “تشويه الذات” (self‑harm). The self‑harm context is associated with the presence of a wound (“جرح”), highlighting the direct tissue damage that can result from intentional self‑inflicted injury. Within the spectrum of intracranial injuries, the detailed diagnosis “Closed fracture of vault of skull with cerebral laceration AND/OR contusion” is classified under two parent categories: “كسر في الجمجمة” (skull fracture) and intracranial injury. This dual classification underscores that the condition involves both a structural fracture of the skull vault and accompanying brain tissue injury, such as laceration or contusion. Together, these relationships illustrate how antemortem injuries are organized from broad, unspecified categories to specific clinical entities, and how the circumstances surrounding the injury—whether assault, self‑harm, or other mechanisms—inform the type and extent of tissue damage observed. Understanding these hierarchical and contextual links aids clinicians in accurately documenting trauma, assessing risk factors, and planning appropriate medical and forensic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 798
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is classified as an إصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury) and is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury). Both Fracture of anterior fossa and Fracture of middle fossa are specific types of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, illustrating that fractures of the anterior and middle cranial fossae fall under the broader category of intracranial injury. The entity إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is also defined as a subtype of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, indicating that closed‑head trauma is encompassed within the same injury classification. Additionally, the term hlava has a relationship with إصابة داخل الجمجمة, further linking it to intracranial injury concepts. Together, these relationships demonstrate how various forms of head trauma—ranging from subarachnoid hemorrhage with open wounds to specific cranial fossa fractures and closed‑head injuries—are organized under the unified concept of intracranial injury, which is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and coordinated patient care.
